text: The laparoscopic view reveals multiple small hepatic cysts and extensive adhesions in the right upper quadrant. The surgeon uses electrocautery and blunt dissection to carefully separate the adhesions before docking the robotic system.
The surgeon inserts an aspiration needle laparoscopically into the posterior liver cyst near segments IV-A and VIII, aspirating thick fluid to confirm the correct location.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to open the cyst wall, creating a small hole and aspirating approximately one liter of brown cystic fluid. The cyst is clearly identified and excised using a robotic vessel sealer. The cyst is visually located adjacent to the falciform ligament and partially attached to the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to carefully dissect and free the liver cyst laterally from the liver parenchyma, achieving minimal to no bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to ligate tissue and bluntly dissect the cyst wall free from the diaphragm, carefully separating the cyst from the surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a robotic suction device to bluntly break apart multiple thin and fragile brown septations within the now mobile liver cyst. After aspirating the septations, the cyst cavity is irrigated, revealing the base of the cyst attached to the liver bed.
The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to dissect and resect portions of the cyst, leaving a small segment of the cyst wall adherent to the liver bed to avoid damaging major hepatic structures.
The surgeon irrigates the area where the liver cyst was excised, inspecting the cavity for signs of bleeding and bile. The liver bed appears hemostatic with no visible bilious drainage.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to bring up the omentum from the lower abdomen and creates an omental patch to cover the large raw surface left after the cystic scission. The patch is formed using the robotic vessel sealer and is approximately the size of the defect.
The surgeon places an omental patch on the liver cyst bed and secures it using a running 3-0 barbed suture, which aids in maintaining hemostasis during the suturing process.
The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer as a needle driver to enhance efficiency during the procedure. The final product is visible, with the raw surface of the diaphragm exposed above.

The surgical team converts back to laparoscopy and places a drain in the right upper quadrant. The angled laparoscopic camera provides a limited view of the area. The cyst is placed in an EndoCatch bag and removed through the twelve millimeter trocar.
